questions.stem: DNA replication produces:

options.text: Exact copies of DNA with identical base sequences

questions.explanation: DNA replication makes exact copies with identical base sequences, needed for reproduction, growth and tissue replacement.

questions.stem: Why is DNA replication required in multicellular organisms?
questions.explanation: DNA replication is required for reproduction and for growth and tissue replacement in multicellular organisms.

questions.scenario: Each new DNA molecule contains one original strand and one new strand.
questions.stem: This describes replication that is:
questions.explanation: DNA replication is semi-conservative: each new molecule has one original (conserved) strand and one new strand.

questions.stem: The accuracy of copying base sequences during replication depends on:
questions.explanation: The semi-conservative process and complementary base pairing allow a high degree of accuracy in copying base sequences.

questions.stem: What is the role of helicase in DNA replication?

options.text: Unwinding DNA and breaking hydrogen bonds between strands

questions.explanation: Helicase unwinds the DNA and breaks the hydrogen bonds between the two strands.

questions.stem: What is the general role of DNA polymerase in replication?

options.text: Adding new nucleotides to build the new strand

questions.explanation: DNA polymerase adds nucleotides to synthesize the new complementary strand.

questions.scenario: A scientist makes many copies of a DNA sample in the laboratory.
questions.stem: Which technique is used?
questions.explanation: PCR amplifies DNA, making many copies using primers, temperature changes and Taq polymerase.

questions.stem: Which heat-tolerant enzyme is used in PCR?
questions.explanation: Taq polymerase, which tolerates the high temperatures used in PCR, synthesizes the new DNA strands.

questions.scenario: DNA fragments are separated in a gel by applying a voltage.
questions.stem: On what basis are the fragments separated in gel electrophoresis?
questions.explanation: Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments by size, as smaller fragments move further through the gel.

questions.stem: Short single strands of DNA that provide a starting point for PCR are called:
questions.explanation: Primers are short sequences that provide a starting point for DNA polymerase during PCR.

questions.stem: Which is an application of PCR and gel electrophoresis?
questions.explanation: Applications include DNA profiling for paternity and forensic investigations.

questions.scenario: In DNA profiling, more genetic markers are examined.
questions.stem: What is the effect of increasing the number of markers?

options.text: It reduces the probability of a false match
It increases the chance of a false match
It has no effect on reliability
It removes the need for DNA

questions.explanation: Increasing the number of markers used in DNA profiling reduces the probability of a false match, enhancing reliability.

questions.stem: The newly synthesized complementary strand reads:
questions.explanation: Complementary base pairing (A-T, G-C) gives ATGC as the complement of TACG.

questions.scenario: A single cell divides to form two cells with identical genetic information.
questions.stem: This required which process beforehand?
questions.explanation: DNA is replicated before cell division so each daughter cell receives an identical copy of the genome.

questions.stem: Helicase breaks which type of bond during replication?
questions.explanation: Helicase breaks the hydrogen bonds between complementary bases, separating the two DNA strands.

questions.stem: DNA profiling can be used to establish:
questions.explanation: DNA profiling has applications including paternity testing and forensic investigations.

questions.scenario: PCR uses repeated cycles of heating and cooling.
questions.stem: Why are temperature changes needed?

options.text: To separate strands, bind primers and allow polymerase to work

questions.explanation: PCR uses temperature changes to separate strands, anneal primers and allow Taq polymerase to extend new strands.

questions.stem: In semi-conservative replication, each original strand acts as a:
questions.explanation: Each original strand serves as a template for synthesizing a new complementary strand.

questions.stem: Increasing the number of measurements in a test generally:
questions.explanation: Reliability is enhanced by increasing the number of measurements, as with markers in DNA profiling.

questions.stem: The two DNA molecules produced by replication of one molecule should be:

options.text: Identical in base sequence to the original

questions.explanation: Replication produces exact copies with base sequences identical to the original DNA molecule.

questions.stem: The polymerase chain reaction is used to:
questions.explanation: PCR amplifies DNA; gel electrophoresis then separates the fragments by size.

questions.stem: Which enzyme is responsible for adding complementary nucleotides during replication?
questions.explanation: DNA polymerase adds nucleotides to build the new strand, while helicase unwinds the DNA.

questions.stem: Why is semi-conservative replication described as accurate?

options.text: Each new strand is built by complementary base pairing with a template

questions.explanation: Building each new strand by complementary base pairing with a conserved template gives high accuracy.

questions.stem: Which pair of techniques is used together in DNA profiling?
questions.explanation: PCR amplifies DNA and gel electrophoresis separates the fragments, together enabling DNA profiling.

questions.stem: DNA replication is essential for reproduction because it:

options.text: Ensures offspring receive a copy of the genetic information

questions.explanation: Replication produces exact copies of DNA so genetic information can be passed on in reproduction.

questions.stem: DNA polymerases add a new nucleotide's 5' end to which end of the growing strand?
questions.explanation: DNA polymerases add the 5' of a new nucleotide to the 3' end of the growing strand, giving directionality.

questions.stem: Replication on the leading strand is:
questions.explanation: The leading strand is replicated continuously; the lagging strand is replicated discontinuously in Okazaki fragments.

questions.scenario: The lagging strand is synthesized in short pieces.
questions.stem: These short pieces are called:
questions.explanation: The lagging strand is made discontinuously as Okazaki fragments, each requiring an RNA primer.

questions.stem: On which strand must RNA primer be added repeatedly?
questions.explanation: The leading strand needs an RNA primer only once, but the lagging strand needs one for each Okazaki fragment.

questions.stem: Which enzyme joins Okazaki fragments together?
questions.explanation: DNA ligase joins Okazaki fragments; primase makes primers and DNA polymerases extend and replace them.

questions.stem: Which enzyme synthesizes the RNA primers in prokaryotic replication?
questions.explanation: DNA primase synthesizes the RNA primers needed to begin DNA synthesis.

questions.stem: In the prokaryotic system, which enzyme carries out most of the new DNA synthesis?
questions.explanation: DNA polymerase III carries out the main DNA synthesis; polymerase I replaces primers, ligase seals gaps and primase makes primers.

questions.stem: DNA polymerase I in prokaryotes is mainly involved in:
questions.explanation: DNA polymerase I removes and replaces the RNA primers with DNA nucleotides.

questions.scenario: A mismatched base is detected at the 3' end of a new strand.
questions.stem: During proofreading, which enzyme removes it?
questions.explanation: DNA polymerase III proofreads by removing a mismatched nucleotide from the 3' terminal and replacing it with a correct one.

questions.stem: DNA proofreading improves replication by:
questions.explanation: Proofreading by DNA polymerase III corrects mismatched bases, greatly increasing the accuracy of replication.

questions.stem: The two ends of a DNA strand are distinguished as:
questions.explanation: A DNA strand has distinct 5' and 3' terminals; polymerases add nucleotides to the 3' end.

questions.stem: Why is the lagging strand synthesized discontinuously?

options.text: DNA polymerase can only add nucleotides 5' to 3', opposite to the strand's direction
It is not copied at all

questions.explanation: Because polymerase works only 5' to 3', the lagging strand is made in short Okazaki fragments away from the replication fork.

questions.stem: Which set of enzymes acts in prokaryotic DNA replication?

options.text: DNA primase, DNA polymerase I, DNA polymerase III and DNA ligase

questions.explanation: Prokaryotic replication involves DNA primase, DNA polymerase I, DNA polymerase III and DNA ligase.

questions.stem: After removing a mismatched nucleotide, DNA polymerase III:

options.text: Replaces it with a correctly matched nucleotide

questions.explanation: Proofreading removes the mismatched base and replaces it with a correctly paired nucleotide.

questions.scenario: The directionality of DNA polymerase determines how each strand is copied.
questions.stem: This directionality is described as:
questions.explanation: DNA polymerases synthesize new DNA in the 5' to 3' direction, adding to the 3' end.

questions.stem: Which term describes replication that produces a strand in one continuous piece?
questions.explanation: The leading strand is synthesized continuously, unlike the discontinuous lagging strand.

questions.stem: Which enzyme seals the gaps between fragments to form a continuous strand?
questions.explanation: DNA ligase seals the gaps between Okazaki fragments, joining them into a continuous lagging strand.

questions.stem: DNA proofreading occurs at which position on the new strand?
questions.explanation: Proofreading by DNA polymerase III acts at the 3' terminal, where a mismatched base is removed and replaced.

questions.stem: Because the two DNA strands are antiparallel, the polymerase copying them must:

options.text: Work in opposite directions on each strand
Work in the same direction on both

questions.explanation: Antiparallel strands and 5'-to-3' synthesis mean replication proceeds oppositely on the leading and lagging strands.

questions.stem: How many RNA primers does the leading strand require?
questions.explanation: The leading strand needs an RNA primer only once, while the lagging strand needs one for each Okazaki fragment.

questions.stem: Proofreading during replication is important because it:
questions.explanation: By correcting mismatches, proofreading keeps replication highly accurate and the mutation rate low.

questions.scenario: The functions of the replication enzymes are studied.
questions.stem: The syllabus limits this detailed enzyme study to which system?
questions.explanation: The functions of DNA primase, polymerases I and III, and ligase are studied in the prokaryotic system.

questions.stem: A new DNA nucleotide is added by joining its 5' phosphate to the:

options.text: 3' hydroxyl of the last nucleotide in the strand

questions.explanation: DNA polymerase adds the 5' of a new nucleotide to the 3' end of the growing strand, hence 5'-to-3' synthesis.

questions.stem: The main difference between leading and lagging strand replication is that the lagging strand is:

options.text: Made discontinuously in Okazaki fragments needing repeated priming

questions.explanation: The lagging strand is synthesized discontinuously as Okazaki fragments, each requiring its own RNA primer.

questions.stem: Which enzyme has the proofreading role described in the syllabus?
questions.explanation: DNA polymerase III proofreads by removing and replacing mismatched nucleotides at the 3' terminal.
